profile.name: Ajit Pawara
profile.title: Cyber Security Student
profile.bio: B.Tech Information Technology student at Government College of Engineering Karad with a strong interest in Cybersecurity and Ethical Hacking. Continuously building expertise through certifications, hands-on projects, and practical experience in web development, Java, and security, with the goal of creating secure and innovative digital solutions.

socials.purpose: Connect on professional networks and follow build-in-public posts
Browse active development repositories and security scripts
Follow daily security logs and personal build-in-public highlights
Micro-blogging updates on security setups and daily training tasks
Join discussions on security engineering, web tech, and learning runs
Hands-on labs and offensive security rooms
Active penetration testing machines and pro labs

projects.title: General Championship Scoreboard
projects.description: Developed a web app for managing a college championship scoreboard with team registrations and updates. Features a real-time database and secure authentication.
projects.contribution: Designed and built the real-time database schema, user authentication logic, and frontend championship scoreboard updates.

projects.title: Personal Portfolio Website
projects.description: Developed a personal portfolio website to showcase projects, achievements, and cybersecurity progress with a sleek hacker aesthetic.
projects.contribution: Created responsive glassmorphic UI components, configured secure settings data forms, and integrated real-time dynamic JSON rendering.

projects.title: Cybersecurity 90-Day Challenge Tracker
projects.description: A comprehensive public logging repository for tracking daily learning progress, labs, and security concepts completed during a 90-day security challenge.
projects.contribution: Authored individual day logs detailing key concepts (CIA triad, subnetting, enumeration) and mapped incident correlations.

projects.title: Library Management System
projects.description: A lightweight web application for managing library catalogues, tracking book issuing, and maintaining member records.
projects.contribution: Designed the cataloging interfaces, user registration layouts, and state management for tracking book status.

projects.title: College Resource Sharing Hub
projects.description: A collaborative student platform for sharing course notes, lecture materials, and academic resources across departments.
projects.contribution: Built dynamic file upload layouts, categorization filters, and resource display grids.

projects.title: BreedSense Dog Classifier
projects.description: An interactive application utilizing web layouts and algorithms to identify and catalog dog breeds with a focus on CSS animations.
projects.contribution: Implemented the styling rules, responsive animal card grids, and transition animations for breed discovery.

projects.title: Database Management System Labs
projects.description: A collection of relational database schemas, SQL query optimizations, and normalization procedures implemented for college lab work.
projects.contribution: Designed schemas, wrote advanced queries, structured tables, and performed normalization tasks.

days.title: What is Cybersecurity + the CIA Triad
days.subtitle: Before any tool, any language, any certificate — every security decision traces back to three words. Today you learn them as a working lens, not a flashcard definition.
days.takeaway: Understand that security is the protection of Confidentiality (privacy), Integrity (accuracy), and Availability (uptime).
days.incidentDetail: Equifax leaked 147M records (Confidentiality breach). Stuxnet modified Iranian centrifuge controllers (Integrity breach). Dyn DNS DDoS took Twitter and Netflix offline (Availability breach).

days.title: Types of Hackers & Attack Categories
days.subtitle: You can't defend what you don't understand. Today: how attackers are categorized by intent and method, using real cases — not movie tropes.
days.takeaway: Classified threat actors by motive and mapped attack workflows using the Cyber Kill Chain model.
days.incidentDetail: Albert Gonzalez stole 170M+ card numbers (Heartland breach). Target attackers used HVAC vendor credentials to breach POS systems over weeks.

days.title: Linux Fundamentals I: Filesystem & Permissions
days.subtitle: Every pentest tool, every SOC server, every CTF box runs on Linux. Today you stop "knowing some commands" and start understanding the system the way attackers and defenders both have to.
days.takeaway: Mastered Linux directory hierarchy, permissions mask (chmod/chown), and the security implications of SUID-root binaries.
days.incidentDetail: Qualys discovered a memory corruption bug in the pkexec SUID-root binary, allowing local privilege escalation on nearly all Linux distributions for 12 years.

days.title: Linux Fundamentals II: Users, Processes & Shell
days.subtitle: Files and permissions were "what's stored." Today is "who's logged in and what's running" — the two questions every SOC analyst asks first when something looks wrong.
days.takeaway: Analyzed background processes, process tree structures, user sessions, and process mimicking evasion techniques.
days.incidentDetail: TeamTNT cryptojackers renamed malicious miner processes to 'kworker' to camouflage them as standard Linux kernel threads, evading visual detection.

days.title: Bash Scripting Basics
days.subtitle: A one-liner you type once is a habit. A script you write once runs a thousand times. Today you write your first real scripts — and learn why a single Bash bug once broke half the internet.
days.takeaway: Wrote automation scripts using loops, conditions, and parsed inputs. Learned the danger of environment variable parsing.
days.incidentDetail: A vulnerability in Bash parsed environment variables poorly, letting remote attackers run arbitrary code over CGI scripts, affecting 500M+ devices.

days.title: Networking I: The OSI Model
days.subtitle: Every packet, every exploit, every Wireshark capture you'll ever look at maps onto 7 layers. Today you stop saying "the network" as one blob and start seeing the stack.
days.takeaway: Mapped network interactions onto the 7-layer OSI model to diagnose where vulnerability vectors and defenses reside.
days.incidentDetail: WannaCry infected systems via EternalBlue, using SMB (Layer 7 Application) over port 445 (Layer 4 Transport) to route across networks (Layer 3).

days.title: Networking II: TCP/IP & Ports
days.subtitle: Yesterday you learned the 7-layer map. Today we slow right down and actually understand the two things that map is built on top of — TCP/IP, and ports — explained in plain language, step by step, so it actually sticks.
days.takeaway: Grasped TCP three-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), state changes, port structures, and port scanner reconnaissance vectors.
days.incidentDetail: Canadian teenager MafiaBoy launched SYN floods to exhaust port connections on major websites like Yahoo, eBay, and CNN, causing $1.2B in damages.

days.title: Networking III: DNS, HTTP/HTTPS & DHCP
days.subtitle: These three protocols run silently behind literally every internet action you take — every website you load, every IP address your device gets assigned. Today you understand what's actually happening, step by step, so you can spot when it's being abused.
days.takeaway: Learned domain hierarchy (DNS resolution), secure data transmission (SSL handshake), and automatic IP configuration (DHCP DORA process).
days.incidentDetail: DNS records for baidu.com were hijacked at the registrar level, redirecting traffic to a page controlled by the Iranian Cyber Army for several hours.

days.title: Subnetting & Routing Basics
days.subtitle: Networks aren't one giant flat blob — they're divided into controlled segments. Today you understand how that division works, why the Target breach happened because of it, and how to use it in labs and interviews.
days.takeaway: Calculated subnet masks (CIDR) and designed segregation protocols to segment POS networks from untrusted contractor segments.
days.incidentDetail: Lack of subnet segmentation between an HVAC contractor's portal and target POS servers allowed attackers to pivot laterally, stealing 40M cards.

days.title: Python for Security I
days.subtitle: Python is the #1 language in cybersecurity — used by attackers, defenders, and bug bounty hunters alike. Today you learn the syntax that matters and write your first actual security-oriented scripts.
days.takeaway: Wrote basic network and parser scripts in Python. Evaluated risks of downloading package dependencies.
days.incidentDetail: Attackers uploaded typosquatted packages like 'request' (instead of requests) to PyPI. Installing them executed scripts to steal tokens/wallets.

days.title: Python for Security II: Sockets & Automation
days.subtitle: Build network tools from scratch using Python sockets — the interface behind port scanners, reverse shells, and automated traffic engines.
days.takeaway: Constructed banner-grabbing and multi-threaded port scanning tools in Python; analyzed offensive automation signatures using the Mirai botnet architecture.
days.incidentDetail: Automated socket-based scanning over Telnet port 23 allowed Mirai to compromise millions of default-configured IoT devices, bringing down DNS providers globally.

days.title: Windows Internals: Registry, Processes & Event Logs
days.subtitle: Discover where attackers hide, how they persist, and how defenders detect them using core Windows Registry paths and default event logs.
days.takeaway: Audited Registry Run keys for persistence, identified core Windows process trees, and parsed Event IDs for logon anomalies and log clearing events.
days.incidentDetail: Malicious code injected into trusted SolarWinds DLLs evaded detection by mimicking normal update processes and abusing weak event log monitoring configurations.

days.title: Virtualization & Your Home Security Lab
days.subtitle: Every lab you've done on TryHackMe runs in someone else's environment. Today you build your own — a personal isolated network where you can break, attack, defend, and experiment freely with zero risk to anything real.
days.takeaway: Designed and deployed an isolated home security lab using VirtualBox with Kali Linux (attacker) and Metasploitable 2 (target) on a Host-Only virtual network; used for authorized vulnerability assessment and penetration testing practice.
days.incidentDetail: A hardware vulnerability in Intel processors that allows a malicious virtual machine running in a guest environment to read memory belonging to the hypervisor or other guest virtual machines.

days.title: Kali Linux Tools: Nmap, Wireshark, Metasploit, Burp Suite
days.subtitle: Day 13 built the lab. Today you actually use it — four tools that appear in every professional penetration test, every SOC investigation, and almost every technical interview for a security role.
days.takeaway: Executed Nmap scans (service/OS/vuln), captured traffic in Wireshark, exploited vsftpd 2.3.4 with Metasploit, and intercepted HTTP requests with Burp Suite on Metasploitable.
days.incidentName: Bangladesh Bank SWIFT Heist / Lazarus Group (2016)
days.incidentDetail: Lazarus Group installed Wireshark inside Bangladesh Bank and captured unencrypted SWIFT traffic for weeks, learning the exact protocol format and authentication flow. They then crafted fraudulent transfer requests that stole $81 million.

days.title: Phase 1 Complete: Review, Quiz & Phase 2 Preview
days.subtitle: Ten days of foundations — from CIA triad to your first exploit. Today you consolidate everything, test yourself, and look ahead at what Phase 2 brings.
days.takeaway: Completed a comprehensive review of Days 1-14, tested knowledge with quiz questions, and previewed Phase 2 (reconnaissance, exploitation, and active directory).
days.incidentDetail: Reviewed CIA triad, hacker types, Linux fundamentals, bash scripting, networking (OSI/TCP/IP/DNS/HTTP/subnetting), Python security, Windows internals, home lab setup, and Kali toolchain.

days.title: Cryptography I: Hashing vs Encryption
days.subtitle: Cryptography is the backbone of everything secure on the internet. Before you can break it, you must understand the three core operations: hashing, symmetric encryption, and asymmetric encryption.
days.takeaway: Understood the difference between hashing (one-way, integrity) and encryption (two-way, confidentiality); identified common hash algorithms and their vulnerabilities.
days.incidentDetail: LinkedIn stored passwords as unsalted SHA-1 hashes. When 167M password hashes were leaked, most were cracked within days using rainbow tables and GPU clusters because SHA-1 was fast and there was no salt.

days.title: Cryptography II: Symmetric vs Asymmetric Encryption
days.subtitle: Symmetric = one key to lock and unlock. Asymmetric = a public key to lock, a private key to unlock. Today you understand when each is used and why the internet needs both.
days.takeaway: Compared AES (symmetric, fast, bulk data) vs RSA (asymmetric, slow, key exchange); learned how TLS handshakes use both to establish secure connections.
days.incidentDetail: A buffer over-read in OpenSSL's Heartbeat extension leaked up to 64KB of server memory per request, including private RSA keys. Attackers could decrypt previously captured TLS traffic. 500K+ servers affected.

days.title: Cryptography III: SSL/TLS Certificates & PKI
days.subtitle: The padlock icon in your browser is backed by one of the most sophisticated trust infrastructures ever created. Today you understand exactly what that certificate means, how a Certificate Authority works, and how the entire system can be subverted.
days.takeaway: Analyzed X.509 certificate chains, understood PKI trust hierarchy, verified certificate revocation, and simulated a self-signed CA in the home lab.
days.incidentDetail: A Dutch Certificate Authority was compromised by attackers who issued 531 fraudulent certificates for domains including google.com, torproject.org, and CIA.gov. Iranian users were MITM'd for months using a fake Google certificate signed by the compromised CA.

days.title: Authentication vs Authorization
days.subtitle: Who are you? and What are you allowed to do? — these are two separate questions that get confused constantly. Today you learn why confusing them is one of the most common root causes of critical CVEs.
days.takeaway: Differentiated authentication (verifying identity) from authorization (granting permissions); analyzed real-world authN/authZ bypass cases including IDOR and broken session management.
days.incidentDetail: A former AWS employee exploited a misconfigured WAF that allowed SSRF to the AWS metadata service. The real vulnerability was authorization — the IAM role attached to the WAF had access to S3 buckets containing 100M+ credit applications. Authentication was fine, but authorization was broken.

days.title: Web Recon: Directory Busting & Technology Fingerprinting
days.subtitle: You know a website's front door exists. Today you find every side-door, maintenance hatch, backup stairwell, and forgotten window — the hidden paths attackers use to bypass logins and inject malware.
days.takeaway: Used gobuster and ffuf for directory enumeration; leveraged WhatWeb and Wappalyzer for passive technology fingerprinting; discovered hidden admin panels and tech stack CVEs.
days.incidentDetail: Magecart Group found an unsecured staging admin path on ba.com via directory busting. They injected a 23-line JavaScript skimmer into the payment page, capturing 380,000 payment card details over 2 weeks. BA was fined £20M under GDPR.

days.title: Java JVM & JDK Architecture
days.subtitle: Java & DSA Masterclass progress log - Day 1
days.takeaway: Understanding bytecode, compilation, and cross-platform compilation logic.
days.incidentDetail: Successfully solved and verified code exercise for Day 1.

days.title: Java Variables & Operators
days.subtitle: Java & DSA Masterclass progress log - Day 2
days.takeaway: Mastered primitive data types vs Reference types, and math operation execution.
days.incidentDetail: Successfully solved and verified code exercise for Day 2.

days.title: Control Flow & Loops
days.subtitle: Java & DSA Masterclass progress log - Day 3
days.takeaway: Implemented conditions, switch cases, and loops (for, while, do-while).
days.incidentDetail: Successfully solved and verified code exercise for Day 3.

days.title: Arrays & Memory Mapping
days.subtitle: Java & DSA Masterclass progress log - Day 4
days.takeaway: Explored 1D/2D arrays allocation in the heap memory and lookup efficiency.
days.incidentDetail: Successfully solved and verified code exercise for Day 4.

days.title: String Manipulation & Pool
days.subtitle: Java & DSA Masterclass progress log - Day 5
days.takeaway: Investigated String immutability and memory optimization via the String Constant Pool.
days.incidentDetail: Successfully solved and verified code exercise for Day 5.

roadmap.title: Syllabus Foundations
roadmap.description: Networking basics (OSI model, TCP/IP, DNS, Subnetting), Linux systems command line, shell permissions, and python script foundations.

roadmap.title: Hands-On Defense & Labs
roadmap.description: Active reconnaissance tools (Nmap, Gobuster), traffic analysis via Wireshark, active directory structures, and SOC monitoring rooms.

roadmap.title: Red vs. Blue Specialization
roadmap.description: Conducting vulnerability assessments, configuring firewalls/IDS, exploiting Web Application flaws (OWASP Top 10), and choosing specialized track.

roadmap.title: Cybersecurity Internship / Role
roadmap.description: Secure a placement as a SOC analyst, red/blue team member, or security engineer.

experience.description: Dedicated to advancing expertise in cyber security through structured learning. Completed an ethical hacking course from Udemy, validating skills and knowledge in the field, and actively pursuing further education to enhance understanding of cybersecurity challenges and solutions.

education.description: Currently in 2nd Year (Semester IV). CGPA: 7.55. Core coursework: Data Structures, Computer Networks, Operating Systems, Database Management Systems, and Security Fundamentals.
